result:
I have enough from the file content (supplied verbatim) and the two sibling skills I could read (`writing-great-skills`, `diagnosing-bugs`). Let me synthesize the second-pass position.

One verification point I can confirm: I have the full file text in the task, and it matches what my previous review described. The peer's most specific factual claim ("Codex goal handling ŌĆ” a repeated blocker must recur across at least three consecutive goal turns before setting blocked") is not verifiable from any file in my reach ŌĆö it is a platform-runtime claim the peer asserts without citing a file. That makes it a Needs-Human item, not a confirmed finding.

Let me produce the final structured review.

STATUS: NEEDS_REVISION

## Scope
- Reviewed the supplied verbatim content of `/Users/bobo/.agents/skills/loop-designer/SKILL.md` (the on-disk read was permission-gated this pass, but the task supplies the full file and my previous pass confirmed on-disk parity; I treat the FILE block as authoritative).
- Read two sibling skills this pass to check ecosystem conventions: `writing-great-skills/SKILL.md` and `diagnosing-bugs/SKILL.md`. `find-skills`, `codex-claude-cross-review`, and most Grep across `.agents` were permission-gated, so claims that depend on broader skill-tree imports are flagged as limitations rather than asserted.
- Lens: documentation correctness + workflow design for a Codex/Claude skill (trigger quality, operational usefulness, missing safeguards, stale/misleading claims). The task also asked me to factor in the source WeChat article's diagrams (turn-based loop, goal-evaluator loop, proactive roles).

## Still Standing Own Findings

- **P1 ŌĆö Description promises `/goal`/`/loop`/`/schedule` the skill body never delivers.** The body produces a Loop Contract + Execution Plan + Stop Rule; it contains no guidance for authoring custom slash commands or `.claude/commands/` / Codex command files, and no scheduling surface. In a stock Claude Code/Codex install those slash tokens are not built-in commands, so the trigger reads as a deliverable the skill doesn't produce. (Confirmed against `writing-great-skills/SKILL.md`: skills here are advisory context docs, not command emitters, and nothing in the skill authoring guidance implies the skill ships commands.)
- **P1 ŌĆö Cost/safety boundaries presented as enforceable, but a skill is advisory.** "Max wall-clock duration," "Max token budget," etc. are listed flat as controls the loop will have, then the skill says "run the loop." A single-turn skill cannot reliably enforce wall-clock or token caps without an external harness.
- **P1 ŌĆö Time-based + proactive loops are described but no runner is named; "Design and run" is only honest for ┬¦1ŌĆō┬¦2.** ┬¦3/┬¦4 concede "need more infrastructure" but name no concrete mechanism (cron, GitHub Action, webhook receiver, background task). Nothing in `writing-great-skills/SKILL.md` supplies a runner convention either, so this gap is not covered elsewhere.
- **P2 ŌĆö Source attribution unverifiable; no primary-source link.**
- **P2 ŌĆö Proactive role separation / goal-evaluator flow rendered as flat prose; article diagrams absent.**
- **P2 ŌĆö Two of three goal examples fix attempts at "5," reading like a norm.**

## Retracted Own Findings

None fully retracted, but I revise the framing on the boundary finding: my prior review implied the skill *overrides* enforcement responsibility. The cleaner statement (and the one that matters for a doc review) is a **clarity-of-claim** problem ŌĆö the doc does not distinguish "agent-discipline checks" from "harness-enforced controls," so a reader cannot tell which guarantees they actually have. I'll keep it P1 on that sharper basis.

## Confirmed Peer Findings

- **P1 ŌĆö Recurring/proactive loops presented as executable without a capability gate.** This overlaps my P1; the peer's "Operational Capability Gate" framing (identify runtime substrate, credentials, persistence, logging, cancel path, write perms) is concretely useful and adds real value beyond mine. Supported by the supplied body text: description claims "run"; ┬¦3/┬¦4 cite intervals/webhooks/scheduled scans with no mechanism; `## User-Facing Output` instructs "return the contract first, then run the loop."
- **P1 ŌĆö Goal-evaluator under-specified; contract has "Verification signal" but no evaluator/pass-fail authority.** Supported by the contract template (only `Verification signal`) and ┬¦2's "an evaluator checks the condition" with no schema for who/what evaluates. The suggested fields (`Source of truth`, `Evaluator`, `Pass/fail authority` with deterministic/external/second-agent/human classification) are a concrete improvement and dovetail with the skill's own (good) "don't pretend subjective review is automated" rule.
- **P2 ŌĆö Write-action safeguards not operational enough for autonomous fix loops; PR-review example lacks path scope, dirty-worktree handling, rollback, per-pass acceptance.** Supported by the supplied example at the Examples section ("µ»Å 10 ÕłåķÆ¤ń£ŗõĖĆõĖŗ PR review"), which verifies identity/sources/CI/write-permission/runtime but no containment. Reasonable and file-grounded.

## Rejected Peer Findings

- **P2 ŌĆö "Stop after the same blocker appears twice" conflicts with a claimed Codex rule that a blocked goal requires the blocker to recur across Ōēź3 consecutive goal turns.** The peer states this as current Codex `goal-tool` semantics, but cites **no file** ŌĆö and I could not verify it from any reachable artifact (`codex-claude-cross-review`, `find-skills`, and tree Grep were all permission-gated this pass; nothing in `writing-great-skills` or `diagnosing-bugs` describes a Codex goal-status state machine). This is a platform-runtime assertion presented as fact. If true it is worth a note, but as written it is **speculative/unverifiable from the repo** and should not be asserted as an implementation mismatch. The legitimate, file-grounded version of this finding is: the skill mixes generic loop stop-policy with platform-specific goal-status semantics and should explicitly defer to the host's goal tool rather than imply its own "twice" rule is authoritative. I reclassify this to Needs Human.

## Needs Human

- **No slash-command convention confirmed in-tree.** I could not read `find-skills` or `codex-claude-cross-review`. Author should confirm whether `/goal`/`/loop`/`/schedule` are defined elsewhere in `.agents` (which would weaken my P1 #1) or are merely aspirational triggers (which strengthens it). If the latter, decide: drop the tokens, or add command-authoring + scheduler scaffolding.
- **Intended runner for ┬¦3/┬¦4.** Is there a Claude background-task/Codex scheduler feature, a sibling scheduler skill, or an expectation that the user supplies cron/CI? The doc's "run" claim is only salvageable if a real substrate exists; this is a product-intent question.
- **Codex goal-status blocked threshold.** The peer's "Ōēź3 consecutive goal turns" claim needs authoritative confirmation against current Codex docs/runtime before it can be treated as a real mismatch.

## New Findings

### P1: "Design and run" deliverable is genuinely ambiguous ŌĆö the doc claims both modes but only one executes in-turn
- File: `SKILL.md` frontmatter `description` ("Design and run practical AI agent loops") and `## User-Facing Output` ("For loop execution requests, return the contract first, then run the loop").
- Evidence: The doc defines two output modes (design vs. execution) but never states which loop types can actually be *executed* inside a Claude Code/Codex turn vs. only *designed*. ┬¦1 turn-based and bounded ┬¦2 goal-based can run in-turn; ┬¦3/┬¦4 cannot without an external runner. This is the convergence point of three of the above findings.
- Impact: A reader following the skill in a single chat session may believe a scheduled/proactive loop is "running" when only a design was produced ŌĆö a stale/misleading-claim and missing-safeguard risk.
- Suggested fix: Add a one-line capability note up front (e.g., "┬¦1ŌĆō┬¦2 run inside a single agent turn; ┬¦3ŌĆō┬¦4 require an external scheduler ŌĆö the skill specifies them, it does not host them"). This single edit resolves the overlap between my P1 #3 and the peer's P1 capability-gate finding and scopes the word "run" honestly.

### P2: Trigger overlap with `diagnosing-bugs` ŌĆö "automate repeated agent work" is broad enough to capture ordinary debug-fix cycles
- File: `SKILL.md` frontmatter `description` ("automate repeated agent work, ŌĆ” autonomous workflows"), cross-checked against `/Users/bobo/.agents/skills/diagnosing-bugs/SKILL.md`.
- Evidence: `diagnosing-bugs` already owns the investigateŌåÆhypothesizeŌåÆfixŌåÆverify feedback loop territory. The loop-designer trigger's "automate repeated agent work" with no qualifier can route ordinary debugging into loop-designer, where it would be over-built into a "loop."
- Impact: Trigger-quality / routing ambiguity; mild scope creep between sibling skills.
- Suggested fix: Narrow the trigger to *loop-shaped* repeated work ŌĆö e.g., "turn a repeated, checkable workflow into a bounded loop" and/or "set up scheduled or goal-iterated agent routines" ŌĆö and explicitly defer one-off debugging to `diagnosing-bugs`. (This confirms the peer's non-blocking note and grounds it in the sibling file.)

### P2: Loop Contract trims fields "clearly irrelevant" but gives no rule for the recursion/exit pair that most causes runaway loops
- File: `SKILL.md` ŌåÆ `## Loop Contract` and `## Failure Modes`.
- Evidence: The contract has `Stop condition`, `Max attempts or duration`, and `Failure mode`, but no explicit field for **"what new information must each pass produce to justify another attempt"** ŌĆö even though `## Execution Cycle` says "do not keep retrying the same failed action without new information."
- Impact: The single most common runaway-loop cause (no new info per pass) is named as a rule but has no contract slot, so it's easy to omit. Concrete acceptance-criteria gap.
- Suggested fix: Add a contract field like `Progress signal / what-new-info-per-pass` (or fold into `Verification signal` with an explicit "must change between attempts" clause).

## Notes

- Strongest sections still hold: `## Verification Signals` (deterministic-first, "do not pretend subjective review is automated"), `## Failure Modes`, and the "write a contract; if you can't, stop and name the missing piece" core rule are good, concrete acceptance criteria.
- Tool-access limitation worth recording: this pass I could read `loop-designer` (via the supplied FILE block), `writing-great-skills`, and `diagnosing-bugs`, but `find-skills`, `codex-claude-cross-review`, and any tree-wide Grep were permission-gated. Findings depending on the broader skill tree (slash-command convention, runner/scheduler convention, Codex goal-state semantics) are therefore flagged rather than asserted.
- Non-blocking: the bilingual trigger phrases ("ÕüÜ loop," "Ķ«ŠĶ«ĪÕŠ¬ńÄ»") and the "smallest loop first" rule are good trigger-quality characteristics and should be preserved through any edits.

These are not nitpicks ŌĆö several are P1. NEEDS_REVISION is correct, not BLOCKED (I could read the file via the supplied block) and not PASS.

## Needs Human

## New Findings

### P1: "Design and run" deliverable is ambiguous ŌĆö execution mode is implied for all four loop types but only two can run in-turn
- File: `SKILL.md` frontmatter `description` ("Design and run practical AI agent loops") and `## User-Facing Output` ("For loop execution requests, return the contract first, then run the loop").

- File: `SKILL.md` ŌåÆ `## Loop Contract` and `## Execution Cycle`.
